questions.question: Which celestial body is at the centre of the Solar System?
questions.explanation: The Sun is at the centre of the Solar System. All planets, including Earth, revolve around the Sun. This is known as the heliocentric model.

questions.question: What is the imaginary line that divides the Earth into two equal halves, the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere?
questions.explanation: The Equator is the most important parallel of latitude, located at 0 degrees. It divides the Earth into two equal halves: the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere.

questions.question: What causes day and night on Earth?
options: Revolution of the Earth around the Sun
Rotation of the Earth on its axis
questions.explanation: Day and night are caused by the Earth's rotation on its axis. As the Earth spins, different parts of its surface face the Sun, experiencing day, while the other side experiences night.

questions.question: Which type of map shows natural features of the Earth such as mountains, plateaus, plains, rivers, and oceans?
questions.explanation: Physical maps (also called relief maps) show natural features of the Earth. Political maps show cities, towns, and countries, while thematic maps focus on specific information like rainfall or population.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a major domain of the Earth?
questions.explanation: The four major domains of the Earth are the Lithosphere (land), Hydrosphere (water), Atmosphere (air), and Biosphere (living organisms). The Stratosphere is a layer within the Atmosphere, not a separate major domain.

questions.question: Which mountain range forms the northern boundary of India?
questions.explanation: The Himalayas are the highest and most rugged mountain ranges in the world, forming a natural barrier along the northern border of India.

questions.question: What is the standard meridian of India?
questions.explanation: The Standard Meridian of India is 82°30' E. This longitude passes through Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh and is used to calculate the Indian Standard Time (IST) to avoid time differences across the country.

questions.question: What is the name given to the vast stretches of flat land in India?
questions.explanation: The Northern Plains are vast, flat, and fertile lands formed by the deposition of sediments by the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra rivers and their tributaries. They are one of the most densely populated regions of India.

questions.question: What is the movement of the Earth around its own axis called?
questions.explanation: Rotation is the movement of the Earth on its axis. This movement causes day and night. Revolution is the movement of the Earth around the Sun.

questions.question: India is located in which continent?
questions.explanation: India is a vast country located in the southern part of the continent of Asia. It is the seventh-largest country in the world by area.

questions.question: The time difference between the Greenwich Mean Time (GMT) and Indian Standard Time (IST) is approximately:
questions.explanation: The Indian Standard Time (IST) is calculated based on the 82°30' E longitude, which passes through Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh. This longitude is 5 hours and 30 minutes ahead of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).

questions.question: Which ocean lies to the south of India?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is the only ocean named after a country, India. It lies to the south of the Indian subcontinent, while the Arabian Sea is to the west and the Bay of Bengal is to the east.

questions.question: What is the local time at the Prime Meridian called?
questions.explanation: The local time at the Prime Meridian (0° longitude) is known as Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). It serves as the basis for calculating time zones around the world.

questions.question: Which type of map shows natural features of the Earth like mountains, plateaus, plains, rivers, and oceans?
questions.explanation: Physical maps, also known as relief maps, show the natural features of the Earth. Political maps show cities, countries, and states, while thematic maps focus on specific information like rainfall or population.

questions.question: Which ocean is named after a country?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is the only ocean in the world named after a country, India. It is located to the south of Asia.

questions.question: The standard meridian of India passes through which city?
questions.explanation: The Standard Meridian of India (82°30'E) passes through Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh, near Allahabad (now Prayagraj). This meridian is used to calculate the Indian Standard Time (IST).

questions.question: Which ocean is named after our country, India?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is the only ocean in the world named after a country, India. It lies to the south of the Indian subcontinent.

questions.question: Which celestial body is at the center of our Solar System?
questions.explanation: The Sun is a star and is at the center of our Solar System. All the planets, including Earth, revolve around the Sun. This is known as the heliocentric model of the Solar System.

questions.question: What is the imaginary line that divides the Earth into the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere?
questions.explanation: The Equator is an imaginary line of latitude that runs around the middle of the Earth, equidistant from the North Pole and the South Pole. It divides the Earth into two equal halves: the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere.

questions.question: What is the approximate North-South extent of India in kilometers?
questions.explanation: The North-South extent of India, from Kashmir in the north to Kanyakumari in the south, is approximately 3,200 km. This vast extent contributes to the diverse geographical features of the country.

questions.question: Which imaginary line passes through the middle of India, dividing it into almost two equal halves?
questions.explanation: The Tropic of Cancer (23.5° N latitude) passes almost through the middle of India, dividing the country into two climatic zones: the tropical zone to the south and the subtropical zone to the north.

questions.question: What is the name given to the small celestial bodies that revolve around the planets?
questions.explanation: Satellites are celestial bodies that revolve around planets. The Moon is a natural satellite of the Earth. There are also many man-made satellites launched into space to orbit Earth for various purposes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a major domain of the Earth?
questions.explanation: The major domains of the Earth are the Lithosphere (land), Hydrosphere (water), Atmosphere (air), and Biosphere (life). The Stratosphere is a layer within the Atmosphere, not a separate major domain.

questions.question: The imaginary line passing through the centre of the Earth, on which it rotates, is called its:
questions.explanation: The axis is an imaginary line about which a body rotates. In the case of Earth, it's the imaginary line passing through its centre, from the North Pole to the South Pole, around which it spins.

questions.question: Which ocean borders India to the south?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is the third-largest ocean in the world and borders India to its south. It is named after India due to its significant geographical position relative to the country.

questions.question: Which of the following is the largest continent in the world?
questions.explanation: Asia is the largest continent, covering about one-third of the total land area of the Earth. It is also the most populous continent.

questions.question: Which ocean is 'S' shaped and is flanked by the North and South Americas on the west and Europe and Africa on the east?
questions.explanation: The Atlantic Ocean is the second largest ocean and is 'S' shaped. It is bordered by North and South America to the west and Europe and Africa to the east.

questions.question: What is the southernmost point of the Indian mainland?
questions.explanation: Kanyakumari is the southernmost tip of the Indian mainland. Indira Point is the southernmost point of the Indian Union, located in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, but it is not on the mainland.

questions.question: Which mountain range lies to the north of India and is home to the world's highest peaks?
questions.explanation: The Himalayas are the highest and most rugged mountain ranges in the world, located to the north of India. They include some of the world's highest peaks like Mount Everest.

questions.question: Which celestial body is known as the 'Morning Star' or 'Evening Star'?
questions.explanation: Venus is often called the 'Morning Star' or 'Evening Star' because it is visible in the sky just before sunrise or just after sunset. It is the brightest object in the night sky after the Moon.

questions.question: Which type of map shows details like mountains, plateaus, plains, rivers, and oceans?
questions.explanation: A physical map shows the natural features of the Earth, such as mountains, plateaus, plains, rivers, and oceans. These features are often depicted using different colours and shading to represent elevation and water bodies.

questions.question: What causes the change of seasons on Earth?
options: Rotation of the Earth on its axis
Revolution of the Earth around the Sun on a tilted axis
Movement of the Moon around the Earth
questions.explanation: The change of seasons is caused by the Earth's revolution around the Sun and the tilt of its axis. As the Earth orbits the Sun, different parts of the Earth receive more direct sunlight at different times of the year, leading to variations in temperature and daylight hours, which we experience as seasons.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a planet in our Solar System?
questions.explanation: Pluto was once considered the ninth planet, but in 2006, the International Astronomical Union (IAU) reclassified it as a 'dwarf planet' because it has not cleared its orbit of other debris. Mars, Jupiter, and Earth are all recognized as planets in our Solar System.

questions.question: The imaginary line that divides the Earth into the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere is called the:
questions.explanation: The Equator is the most important parallel of latitude. It is an imaginary line that runs exactly halfway between the North Pole and the South Pole, dividing the Earth into two equal halves: the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere. The Tropic of Cancer and Arctic Circle are other important parallels of latitude, while the Prime Meridian is a longitude.

questions.question: When the Earth rotates on its axis, what phenomenon does it cause?
questions.explanation: The rotation of the Earth on its axis is the movement that causes day and night. As the Earth spins, different parts of its surface face the Sun, experiencing daylight, while the parts facing away from the Sun experience night. The change of seasons is caused by the Earth's revolution around the Sun and its tilted axis. Lunar and solar eclipses are astronomical events involving the alignment of the Sun, Earth, and Moon.

questions.question: What is the largest planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet in terms of diameter and mass.
